texts.label: text
texts.orig: Styrene-butadiene  rubber/natural  rubber  blend  membranes  were  prepared by conventional vulcanization technique. The morphology of styrenebutadiene  rubber/natural  rubber  blend  membranes  has  been  investigated by combining  scanning  electron  microscopy  with  measurements  of  permeability to  chlorinated  hydrocarbon  vapours.  The  results  obtained  from both  SEM  and permeation measurements suggest that SBR/NR blend membranes are heterophase  in  nature.  The  effect of  structure  of  the  penetrant  on  permeation through  blend  membranes  was  also  investigated.  Finally,  the  permeation of chlorohydrocarbon  vapours  through  membranes  is compared  with  permeation of  oxygen  and  nitrogen  gases  through  the  membranes.
texts.text: Styrene-butadiene  rubber/natural  rubber  blend  membranes  were  prepared by conventional vulcanization technique. The morphology of styrenebutadiene  rubber/natural  rubber  blend  membranes  has  been  investigated by combining  scanning  electron  microscopy  with  measurements  of  permeability to  chlorinated  hydrocarbon  vapours.  The  results  obtained  from both  SEM  and permeation measurements suggest that SBR/NR blend membranes are heterophase  in  nature.  The  effect of  structure  of  the  penetrant  on  permeation through  blend  membranes  was  also  investigated.  Finally,  the  permeation of chlorohydrocarbon  vapours  through  membranes  is compared  with  permeation of  oxygen  and  nitrogen  gases  through  the  membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Vapour permeation has emerged as a new industrial technology. In vapour permeation, transport of a condensable vapour through a dense membrane  consecutive  to  an  activity  gradient  takes  place.  This  process  offers the  unique feature  of  studying  the  transport process  of  a  single permeant through a dense membrane under various upstream activities l \ l . Such characteristics can by no means be obtained by liquid permeation (pervaporation) where the modification of the upstream activity of component can only be achieved by adding another compound to the mixture; the activity of both the  components is  modified in this case in compliance  with  the  Gibbs-Duhem  relation,  which  complicates  the  transport analysis.  On  the  other  hand,  coupling  phenomena  are  not  to  be  considered with pure vapour permeation results. In the case of pure permeation, upstream activity can be easily calculated; provided upstream pressure is precisely  monitored  111.
texts.text: Vapour permeation has emerged as a new industrial technology. In vapour permeation, transport of a condensable vapour through a dense membrane  consecutive  to  an  activity  gradient  takes  place.  This  process  offers the  unique feature  of  studying  the  transport process  of  a  single permeant through a dense membrane under various upstream activities l \ l . Such characteristics can by no means be obtained by liquid permeation (pervaporation) where the modification of the upstream activity of component can only be achieved by adding another compound to the mixture; the activity of both the  components is  modified in this case in compliance  with  the  Gibbs-Duhem  relation,  which  complicates  the  transport analysis.  On  the  other  hand,  coupling  phenomena  are  not  to  be  considered with pure vapour permeation results. In the case of pure permeation, upstream activity can be easily calculated; provided upstream pressure is precisely  monitored  111.

texts.orig: Besides  these  advantages,  a  study  of  solvent  vapour  permeation direct practical conclusions for the understanding and rational design volatile  organic  components  (VOC)  vapour  recovery  from  contaminated stream ß,Al. Vapour permeation also offers significant opportunities energy saving and solvent reuse, compared to classical VOC processes  such  as  incineration,  oxidation  or  active  carbon  absorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  addition  to  these  advantages,  vapour  sorption  technique  is  a  good  tool for  the  thermodynamic  characterization  of  polymer  blends 15,  61. Analysis  of equilibrium sorption of a vapour by the blend can provide information regarding  polymer  interactions.  The  amount  of  vapour  sorbed  is  related  to  its interaction  with  the  blend.  The  usual  technique  to  investigate  the  morphology of an immiscible polymer blend is  scanning electron microscopy (SEM) applied to the surface of cryofactures. However, that technique gives a picture of only a minute portion of the sample that is  sometimes nonrepresentative  of  the  bulk  of  the  material.  Moreover,  from  a  two-dimensional picture  it  is  not  easy  to  estimate  when  phase  inversion  occurs  as  a  function  of composition and when the level of co-continuity reaches a maximum. Recently  Geuskens  111 found  that  scanning  electron  microscopic  analysis  and permeability measurements were complementary sources of information about  the  morphology  of  the  polyethylene-silicone  blends.
texts.text: In  addition  to  these  advantages,  vapour  sorption  technique  is  a  good  tool for  the  thermodynamic  characterization  of  polymer  blends 15,  61. Analysis  of equilibrium sorption of a vapour by the blend can provide information regarding  polymer  interactions.  The  amount  of  vapour  sorbed  is  related  to  its interaction  with  the  blend.  The  usual  technique  to  investigate  the  morphology of an immiscible polymer blend is  scanning electron microscopy (SEM) applied to the surface of cryofactures. However, that technique gives a picture of only a minute portion of the sample that is  sometimes nonrepresentative  of  the  bulk  of  the  material.  Moreover,  from  a  two-dimensional picture  it  is  not  easy  to  estimate  when  phase  inversion  occurs  as  a  function  of composition and when the level of co-continuity reaches a maximum. Recently  Geuskens  111 found  that  scanning  electron  microscopic  analysis  and permeability measurements were complementary sources of information about  the  morphology  of  the  polyethylene-silicone  blends.

texts.orig: The styrene-butadiene rubber used was synaprene (1502) with 25% styrene content supplied by Synthetic and Chemicals Ltd., Bareilly, U.P, India. Natural rubber (ISNR-5) was supplied by The Rubber Research Institute  of  India,  Kottayam,  Kerala.  The  solvents  CH 2 CI2,  CHCI3,  and  CCI4 (Merck  India  Ltd.,  Ltd.,  Mumbai)  were  used  without  further  purification.  All other  rubber  ingredients  were  of  laboratory  reagent  grade,  supplied  by  Bayer India  Ltd.,  Mumbai,  India.
texts.text: The styrene-butadiene rubber used was synaprene (1502) with 25% styrene content supplied by Synthetic and Chemicals Ltd., Bareilly, U.P, India. Natural rubber (ISNR-5) was supplied by The Rubber Research Institute  of  India,  Kottayam,  Kerala.  The  solvents  CH 2 CI2,  CHCI3,  and  CCI4 (Merck  India  Ltd.,  Ltd.,  Mumbai)  were  used  without  further  purification.  All other  rubber  ingredients  were  of  laboratory  reagent  grade,  supplied  by  Bayer India  Ltd.,  Mumbai,  India.

texts.orig: Styrene-butadiene rubber/natural rubber blend membranes (SBR/NR) with varying  composition denoted as N0, N30, N50, N70 and N,00 (where subscript  represent  the  weight  percent  of  natural  rubber)  were  prepared by master  batch  technique.  The  formulation  of  mixes  used  is  given  in  Table 1. The  mixing  was  done  in  a  two  roll  mixing  having  the  friction  ratio  (1:1.4)  at 30°C.  The  curing  was  done  on  a  hydraulic  press  at  150°C  for  their  optimum cure  time.  Optimum  cure  time  is defined  as  the  cure  time  corresponding  to  90 percent of the maximum torque. It is the vulcanization time to obtain optimum  physical  properties  and  is calculated  using  the  equation

texts.orig: Samples for  SEM studies were cryogenically fractured under liquid nitrogen.  The  fractured  samples  were  then  sputter  coated  with  gold  and the photographs  were  taken  on  a  Phillips's  model  scanning  electron  microscope.

texts.orig: The vapour permeability was determined at 30° C by measuring the weight loss of small vials filled with solvents and tightly closed by a membrane  of  150  μιη  thickness.  The  weight  loss  is  proportional  to  time,  area of  the  membranes  and  pressure  inside  the  vials  and  is  inversely proportional to  the  thickness  of  the  membrane.
texts.text: The vapour permeability was determined at 30° C by measuring the weight loss of small vials filled with solvents and tightly closed by a membrane  of  150  μιη  thickness.  The  weight  loss  is  proportional  to  time,  area of  the  membranes  and  pressure  inside  the  vials  and  is  inversely proportional to  the  thickness  of  the  membrane.

texts.orig: A circular sample of the dry  membrane was vacuum dried at room temperature  (30°C)  for 24  h.  It  was  immersed  in  chlorohydrocarbons  taken  in diffusion bottles and kept in a thermostatically controlled oven. After attaining  equilibrium,  it  was  again  taken  out  and  the  solvent  adhered  to  the sample  was  removed  by  means  of  a  tissue  paper  and  quickly  weighed  on  an electronic  balance  with  an  accuracy  of  0.02g.  Since  the  weighings  were  done within  30s,  the  error  associated  with  the  evaporation  of  solvents  is  negligible. The  experiments  were  duplicated  or  triplicated in  most  cases.  The sorption coefficient (S)  can  be  calculated  from the  following  equation

It  was  immersed  in  chlorohydrocarbons  taken  in diffusion bottles and kept in a thermostatically controlled oven. After attaining  equilibrium,  it  was  again  taken  out  and  the  solvent  adhered  to  the sample  was  removed  by  means  of  a  tissue  paper  and  quickly  weighed  on  an electronic  balance  with  an  accuracy  of  0.02g.  Since  the  weighings  were  done within  30s,  the  error  associated  with  the  evaporation  of  solvents  is  negligible. The  experiments  were  duplicated  or  triplicated in  most  cases.  The sorption coefficient (S)  can  be  calculated  from the  following  equation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeation of organic vapors through polymeric membranes is usually  assumed  to  follow  a  solution  diffusion  process.  Thus  the  permeability (P) is  the  product  of  sorption coefficient  (S)  and  diffusion  coefficient  (D) /8-11/.
texts.text: The permeation of organic vapors through polymeric membranes is usually  assumed  to  follow  a  solution  diffusion  process.  Thus  the  permeability (P) is  the  product  of  sorption coefficient  (S)  and  diffusion  coefficient  (D) /8-11/.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeability through a  polymer membrane  depends  on  several factors such as nature of the polymer, nature of the penetrant and the process conditions  /12-14/.  A  detailed analysis  of  the  transport process  of  a single permeant can be achieved, provided sorption and either diffusion or permeation can be studied independently. Diffusion coefficients are in principle  assessable  from  sorption  measurements.  It  has  been  suggested  that effective diffusion coefficients obtained from permeability and sorption measurements are  more realistic for a correct transport description /15/.
texts.text: Permeability through a  polymer membrane  depends  on  several factors such as nature of the polymer, nature of the penetrant and the process conditions  /12-14/.  A  detailed analysis  of  the  transport process  of  a single permeant can be achieved, provided sorption and either diffusion or permeation can be studied independently. Diffusion coefficients are in principle  assessable  from  sorption  measurements.  It  has  been  suggested  that effective diffusion coefficients obtained from permeability and sorption measurements are  more realistic for a correct transport description /15/.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Permeability  through  a  polymer  membrane  depends  upon  the  solubility and diffusivity  of  the  solvent  molecule  in  the  membrane,  at  given temperature, pressure  and  concentration.
texts.text: Permeability  through  a  polymer  membrane  depends  upon  the  solubility and diffusivity  of  the  solvent  molecule  in  the  membrane,  at  given temperature, pressure  and  concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These  observations  support  the  heterophase  structure  revealed from  the morphology.  Figure  3  shows  the  morphology  of  SBR/NR  blends.  The  SEM photographs  show  that  blends  are  [Figure  (3a)]  heterogeneous  in  nature.  In N30  blend  NR  is  dispersed  as  domains  in  the  continuous  SBR  matrix.  The average  domain  size  of  the  dispersed  particles  is  3.2  μιτι.  In  Figure  3(b)  and 3(c)  [(N 50 )  and  (N70 )  respectively]  NR  becomes  continuous  and  the  system exhibits  a  co-continuous  morphology.  The  dispersed  particle  morphology  of N30  blend  offers a  more  tortuous  path  for the  penetrants  thereby  reducing  the diffusivity  through  the  membrane,  which in  turn results  in  the  reduction in permeability coefficient. In both N30 and N70 [Figure 3(b) and 3(c) respectively] NR becomes continuous and the system exhibits a cocontinuous  morphology.  The  co-continuous  nature  of  the  N 50 and  N 70 blend offers  a  smooth passage  for  the  penetrants  through  the  membrane, thereby showing  increased  permeability.
texts.text: These  observations  support  the  heterophase  structure  revealed from  the morphology.  Figure  3  shows  the  morphology  of  SBR/NR  blends.  The  SEM photographs  show  that  blends  are  [Figure  (3a)]  heterogeneous  in  nature.  In N30  blend  NR  is  dispersed  as  domains  in  the  continuous  SBR  matrix.  The average  domain  size  of  the  dispersed  particles  is  3.2  μιτι.  In  Figure  3(b)  and 3(c)  [(N 50 )  and  (N70 )  respectively]  NR  becomes  continuous  and  the  system exhibits  a  co-continuous  morphology.  The  dispersed  particle  morphology  of N30  blend  offers a  more  tortuous  path  for the  penetrants  thereby  reducing  the diffusivity  through  the  membrane,  which in  turn results  in  the  reduction in permeability coefficient. In both N30 and N70 [Figure 3(b) and 3(c) respectively] NR becomes continuous and the system exhibits a cocontinuous  morphology.  The  co-continuous  nature  of  the  N 50 and  N 70 blend offers  a  smooth passage  for  the  penetrants  through  the  membrane, thereby showing  increased  permeability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The three chlororohydrocarbons, viz, CH2C12 CHC13  and CCI4, are chosen  in  the  increasing  order  of  their  molecular  size  to  study  the  effect  of structure  of  the  penetrant  on  the  permeation properties. The permeability coefficient  is  decreased with increasing chlorine content in the penetrant whereas  the  sorption  coefficient  is  increased  with  increase  of  chlorine  content [Figures 1  and 2]. The influencing  factor is the difference  in solubility parameter  between  the  polymer  and  solvent.
texts.text: The three chlororohydrocarbons, viz, CH2C12 CHC13  and CCI4, are chosen  in  the  increasing  order  of  their  molecular  size  to  study  the  effect  of structure  of  the  penetrant  on  the  permeation properties. The permeability coefficient  is  decreased with increasing chlorine content in the penetrant whereas  the  sorption  coefficient  is  increased  with  increase  of  chlorine  content [Figures 1  and 2]. The influencing  factor is the difference  in solubility parameter  between  the  polymer  and  solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  permeability  coefficient  decreases  with  increase  of  molecule  size  of the  penetrant  whereas  the  sorption  coefficient  increases  with  increase  of  the penetrant  size  [Figures 1 and  2],  The  sorption  coefficient  increases  because the difference in solubility parameter of the solvent and polymer (Δδ) decreases  in the  order  CH 2 CI2>  CHCI3>  CCI4  [ Δδ  CH 2 C12  =  1.525.Δ5  CHCI3 =  1.125,  Δδ CCI4 =  0.425]  of  molecular  size.  The  solubility  parameter  is  an index  of  interaction  between  the  polymer  and  solvent.
texts.text: The  permeability  coefficient  decreases  with  increase  of  molecule  size  of the  penetrant  whereas  the  sorption  coefficient  increases  with  increase  of  the penetrant  size  [Figures 1 and  2],  The  sorption  coefficient  increases  because the difference in solubility parameter of the solvent and polymer (Δδ) decreases  in the  order  CH 2 CI2>  CHCI3>  CCI4  [ Δδ  CH 2 C12  =  1.525.Δ5  CHCI3 =  1.125,  Δδ CCI4 =  0.425]  of  molecular  size.  The  solubility  parameter  is  an index  of  interaction  between  the  polymer  and  solvent.

texts.orig: The permeability of immiscible polymer blends depends on their morphology. They  can  be  described  as  fully continuous  and

The permeability of such systems varies with permeabilities of Px and P2 of the individual components and with their  volume fractions φ, and  <J>2 . If  the continuous phase  is  more  permeable,  the  parallel  model  represents  the  limiting  behavior, which  is  given  by  the  equation  /16/.

texts.orig: A  plot  o f  permeability  vs.  volume  fraction  shows  an  upward  concavity  for parallel  and  a  downward  concavity  for  series  model.  In  polymer  blends  with more  complex  morphologies,  change  of  concavity  is  also  expected  to  occur with  phase  inversion  when  a  dispersed  phase  becomes  continuous  and  vice versa.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4: Comparison of experimental permeability values with theoretical permeability  values
texts.text: Fig. 4: Comparison of experimental permeability values with theoretical permeability  values

texts.label: text
texts.orig: data  at  30  wt.  %  of  NR  is close  to  series  model.  The  behaviour  of  N 30 blend  is associated with the  hetrophase morphology  where  NR is  dispersed in the continuous  SBR  matrix.  Because  of  this  morphology,  the  permeation  path  is more complicated. The experimental data lies  below all other theoretical models. This supports the theory that more permeable NR forms the dispersed  phase  in  the  continuous  matrix.  The  curves  show  a  weak  inflection point at volume fraction > 0.3. This probably coincides with the phase inversion. The behaviour of  N50 and N70 blends are associated with cocontinuous  morphology  where  both  phases  exist  as  co-continuous. Because of  this,  the  permeation path is  more  smooth. Therefore  experimental data merges  with  models  like  parallel,  Maxwell  and  Robeson  at  volume  fraction of  0.5  and  0.7.  These  points  correspond  to  the  maximum  co-continuity  in  the system.
texts.text: data  at  30  wt.  %  of  NR  is close  to  series  model.  The  behaviour  of  N 30 blend  is associated with the  hetrophase morphology  where  NR is  dispersed in the continuous  SBR  matrix.  Because  of  this  morphology,  the  permeation  path  is more complicated. The experimental data lies  below all other theoretical models. This supports the theory that more permeable NR forms the dispersed  phase  in  the  continuous  matrix.  The  curves  show  a  weak  inflection point at volume fraction > 0.3. This probably coincides with the phase inversion. The behaviour of  N50 and N70 blends are associated with cocontinuous  morphology  where  both  phases  exist  as  co-continuous. Because of  this,  the  permeation path is  more  smooth. Therefore  experimental data merges  with  models  like  parallel,  Maxwell  and  Robeson  at  volume  fraction of  0.5  and  0.7.  These  points  correspond  to  the  maximum  co-continuity  in  the system.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3 Comparison  of vapour transport with  gas  transport
texts.text: 3.3 Comparison  of vapour transport with  gas  transport

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Transport  of  oxygen  and  nitrogen  gases  through  SBR/NR  blends  /17/  is compared  with  transport  of  CH2C12 vapours  through  SBR/NR  blends  [Table 2].  The  permeability  of  oxygen  and  nitrogen  gases  through  SBR/NR  blends  is higher  compared  with  the  permeability  of  vapours  through  the  blends.  These gas  ( 0 2 and  N2) transport  studies  were  performed  under  a  feed  pressure  of  40 psi  and  therefore  the  permeation  values  are  much  higher  by  several  orders  of magnitude.  On  the  other  hand  vapour  transport  experiments  were  performed under  atmospheric  pressure.  Therefore  the  permeation  values  are  lower  than those  of  gas  permeation.
texts.text: Transport  of  oxygen  and  nitrogen  gases  through  SBR/NR  blends  /17/  is compared  with  transport  of  CH2C12 vapours  through  SBR/NR  blends  [Table 2].  The  permeability  of  oxygen  and  nitrogen  gases  through  SBR/NR  blends  is higher  compared  with  the  permeability  of  vapours  through  the  blends.  These gas  ( 0 2 and  N2) transport  studies  were  performed  under  a  feed  pressure  of  40 psi  and  therefore  the  permeation  values  are  much  higher  by  several  orders  of magnitude.  On  the  other  hand  vapour  transport  experiments  were  performed under  atmospheric  pressure.  Therefore  the  permeation  values  are  lower  than those  of  gas  permeation.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 2 Comparison  of gas transport  with  vapour  transport
texts.text: Table 2 Comparison  of gas transport  with  vapour  transport

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In SBR/NR blends, SEM and permeability measurements are complementary  sources  of  information  about  the  morphology  of  the  blends. Investigation of permeability data in SBR/NR blends shows that the permeation coefficients increase with volume fraction of NR. This is attributed  to  the  increased  sorption  coefficients  with  volume  fraction  of  NR. In  comparing  the  permeability  of  different blend  compositions,  it  is  seen  that the N30 blend has the lowest permeability values and N70 blend has the highest.  This  behaviour  has  been  attributed  to  the  heterogeneous  nature  of  the N30 blend and the co-continuous nature of the N70 blend respectively. Comparison of the experimental permeability data supports the heterogeneous  nature  of  the  blend.  The  permeability  of  oxygen  and nitrogen gases  through  SBR/NR  blends  is  higher  compared  with  the  permeability of chlorohydrocarbon  vapours  through  the  blends.  This  is  due  to  the  fact  that gas  ( 0 2 and  N 2 )  transport  studies  were  performed  under  a  feed  pressure  of  40 psi  whereas  vapour  transport  experiments  were  performed  under  atmospheric pressure.  The  effect  of  structure  of  the  penetrant  on  permeation properties was  examined,  and  it was  found  that  the  permeability  coefficient  is  decreased whereas  the  sorption  coefficient  is  increased  when  the  chlorine  content  in  the penetrant is increased. This is because of the difference in solubility parameter  between  the  polymer  and  solvent.
texts.text: In SBR/NR blends, SEM and permeability measurements are complementary  sources  of  information  about  the  morphology  of  the  blends. Investigation of permeability data in SBR/NR blends shows that the permeation coefficients increase with volume fraction of NR. This is attributed  to  the  increased  sorption  coefficients  with  volume  fraction  of  NR. In  comparing  the  permeability  of  different blend  compositions,  it  is  seen  that the N30 blend has the lowest permeability values and N70 blend has the highest.  This  behaviour  has  been  attributed  to  the  heterogeneous  nature  of  the N30 blend and the co-continuous nature of the N70 blend respectively. Comparison of the experimental permeability data supports the heterogeneous  nature  of  the  blend.  The  permeability  of  oxygen  and nitrogen gases  through  SBR/NR  blends  is  higher  compared  with  the  permeability of chlorohydrocarbon  vapours  through  the  blends.  This  is  due  to  the  fact  that gas  ( 0 2 and  N 2 )  transport  studies  were  performed  under  a  feed  pressure  of  40 psi  whereas  vapour  transport  experiments  were  performed  under  atmospheric pressure.  The  effect  of  structure  of  the  penetrant  on  permeation properties was  examined,  and  it was  found  that  the  permeability  coefficient  is  decreased whereas  the  sorption  coefficient  is  increased  when  the  chlorine  content  in  the penetrant is increased. This is because of the difference in solubility parameter  between  the  polymer  and  solvent.
